title: Capacity, Inventory & Planning and Control
simple_html:
Once an operation is designed, it must be run day to day: matching how much it can do (capacity) to how much is demanded, deciding how much stock to hold (inventory), and scheduling the work (planning and control). These three jobs keep supply and demand in balance.

Capacity: how much you can produce

Capacity is the maximum output an operation can achieve in a period. The challenge is that demand varies, so managers choose a strategy: level (keep output steady and absorb swings with stock), chase (flex output up and down to match demand), or demand management (shift demand itself, e.g. with off-peak pricing). Each balances cost, service, and inventory differently.

Inventory: how much stock to hold

Inventory is stored materials or goods. It buffers against uncertainty and smooths flow, but it ties up cash and can hide problems. Two classic questions: how much to order (the Economic Order Quantity, which balances ordering cost against holding cost) and when to order (the reorder point, which includes safety stock for uncertainty).

Planning and control: scheduling the work

This decides what gets made or done, when, and in what order, matching the operation's resources to actual demand over time.

Key takeaways

  • The core task is matching supply (capacity) with demand over time.
  • Inventory is a buffer: useful, but expensive and a place where problems hide.
  • EOQ tells you the order size that minimises total cost; the reorder point tells you when to order.
  • Capacity strategy (level vs chase) trades off cost, inventory, and customer service.
intermediate_html:
This lesson covers the running of an operation: ensuring it has the right capacity, holds the right inventory, and uses planning and control to reconcile supply with demand over time. These are the day-to-day levers that turn a well-designed process (Lesson 4) into reliable output.

Capacity management

Capacity is the maximum level of value-added activity an operation can sustain. Measured capacity has layers: design capacity (theoretical max), effective capacity (after planned losses like maintenance and changeovers), and actual output (after unplanned losses). Two ratios follow: utilisation = actual output / design capacity, and efficiency = actual output / effective capacity. The combined measure for equipment is OEE (availability x performance x quality).

Because demand fluctuates, managers choose among three pure capacity strategies, usually blended:

  • Level capacity: hold output constant, absorbing demand swings with inventory (or queues in services). Smooth and efficient, but builds stock or makes customers wait.
  • Chase demand: vary output to track demand (overtime, temps, subcontracting). Low inventory but costly and hard in capital-intensive operations.
  • Demand management: influence demand to fit capacity (off-peak pricing, appointments, promotions in troughs).

Inventory management

Inventory exists to decouple stages and buffer uncertainty, but it consumes working capital and (in lean thinking, Lesson 7) hides problems. The classic ordering question splits in two:

How much to order, the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ): EOQ minimises the sum of ordering costs and holding costs. EOQ = sqrt(2 D S / H), where D = annual demand, S = cost per order, H = annual holding cost per unit. Order too little and you re-order constantly (high ordering cost); order too much and you pay to hold stock (high holding cost). EOQ is the balance point.

When to order, the reorder point (ROP): ROP = (demand per day x lead time) + safety stock. Safety stock covers variability in demand and lead time, sized to a target service level (e.g. enough that you stock out only 5% of cycles).

Planning and control

Planning sets intentions; control reacts to reality. Approaches sit on a spectrum: MRP (Material Requirements Planning) is a push system that explodes a master schedule and bill of materials into timed orders; JIT/lean (Lesson 7) is a pull system where downstream demand pulls work forward via kanban. Little's Law links the three flow measures: Inventory = throughput rate x flow time, a powerful relationship for diagnosing where work is piling up.

Worked example (EOQ)

A retailer sells 10,000 units a year (D). Each order costs $50 to place (S), and holding one unit for a year costs $4 (H). EOQ = sqrt((2 x 10,000 x 50)/4) = sqrt(250,000) = 500 units per order. So it should order 500 units at a time, placing 20 orders a year. If daily demand is ~40 units and lead time is 5 days, the base reorder point is 200 units; adding, say, 80 units of safety stock for a 95% service level gives ROP = 280 units.

How to apply it

(1) Forecast demand and its variability; (2) measure capacity (design/effective/OEE) and choose a level/chase/management blend; (3) classify inventory (e.g. ABC analysis) and set EOQ and ROP for important items; (4) pick a planning approach (MRP push vs lean pull) that fits the process type; (5) use Little's Law to monitor flow. Together these keep service high while controlling the cash tied up in capacity and stock, the perennial operations balancing act.
full_html:
Planning and control is the reconciliation of supply with demand in the short and medium term, the activity that runs the operation designed in Lesson 4 within the strategic envelope of Lesson 3. It rests on three interlocking domains: capacity (how much the operation can do), inventory (the buffers it holds), and the planning and control systems that schedule work. This lesson develops each, the quantitative models that govern them (OEE, EOQ, reorder points, safety stock, Little's Law), and the push-versus-pull debate that connects to lean.

Capacity management

Capacity is the maximum level of value-added activity over a period that the process can achieve under normal operating conditions. It is measured in layers: design capacity (the theoretical maximum), effective capacity (design less planned losses, maintenance, changeovers, scheduling constraints), and actual output (effective less unplanned losses, breakdowns, quality failures). From these come utilisation (actual / design) and efficiency (actual / effective). For equipment, these aggregate into Overall Equipment Effectiveness: OEE = availability x performance x quality, a diagnostic that decomposes losses into their three sources (Nakajima, 1988, in the TPM tradition).

Capacity planning addresses three time horizons, long-term (structural, the capacity decision area of operations strategy, including timing strategies of lead, lag, or match), medium-term (aggregate planning), and short-term (scheduling). The medium-term problem, coping with fluctuating demand, admits three pure responses (Slack et al.), almost always blended:

  • Level capacity: constant output; demand-supply mismatch is absorbed by finished-goods inventory (manufacturing) or by queues and backlogs (services). Maximises utilisation and stability but accumulates inventory or wait, costly and, in lean terms, wasteful.
  • Chase demand: output flexed to track demand via overtime, hiring/firing, part-time and temporary labour, and subcontracting. Minimises inventory but raises labour and coordination cost and is impractical for capital-intensive continuous processes.
  • Demand management: shaping demand to fit capacity, off-peak pricing (revenue management), appointment systems, promotions in troughs, and product/service ranges with counter-seasonal demand.

The choice is fundamentally a trade-off among inventory cost, capacity (labour) cost, and customer service, the same tension that recurs throughout OM.

Inventory management

Inventory is any stored transformed resource. It exists for legitimate reasons, buffer/safety stock against uncertainty, cycle stock from batching, anticipation stock for known future demand, and pipeline stock in transit, but it consumes working capital, incurs holding cost, risks obsolescence, and, per Ohno (1988) and the lean school (Lesson 7), conceals process problems (the "sea of inventory" metaphor). Inventory management therefore seeks the minimum stock consistent with the target service level.

How much to order, the Economic Order Quantity. The EOQ model (Harris, 1913) finds the order quantity Q that minimises total annual cost, the sum of ordering cost (D/Q x S) and holding cost (Q/2 x H). Differentiating and solving gives EOQ = sqrt(2DS/H), where D = annual demand, S = ordering (setup) cost, H = annual holding cost per unit. The model's robustness, total cost is flat near the optimum, makes it durable despite restrictive assumptions (constant demand, fixed lead time, no stockouts). Extensions handle quantity discounts (compare total cost at the EOQ and at each price-break quantity) and production runs (the EPQ/economic production quantity). Crucially, EOQ reveals that reducing setup/ordering cost S shrinks the optimal batch, the analytical justification for SMED and small-batch lean production (Lesson 7).

When to order, the reorder point and safety stock. Under continuous review, an order is placed when stock falls to the reorder point: ROP = (average demand during lead time) + safety stock. Safety stock buffers variability; for variable demand with a service-level target it is SS = z x sigma_d x sqrt(LT), where z is the service-level factor (e.g. 1.65 for 95%), sigma_d the standard deviation of demand per period, and LT the lead time. Higher service levels require disproportionately more safety stock, the diminishing return that drives ABC prioritisation (Pareto, after Dickie's ABC classification): lavish service on the vital few high-value A-items, economise on the trivial many C-items.

Planning and control systems: push versus pull

Planning sets intended activity; control monitors and re-plans against reality, the loop is supervised through loading, sequencing, scheduling, and monitoring. Two philosophies anchor the spectrum:

  • MRP (Material Requirements Planning), a push system (Orlicky, 1975): a master production schedule is exploded through the bill of materials and inventory records into timed, netted purchase and production orders. Extended to MRP II and ERP, it excels with dependent, lumpy demand and complex BOMs but is sensitive to data accuracy and forecast error.
  • JIT / Lean, a pull system (Ohno, 1988): downstream consumption authorises upstream replenishment, signalled by kanban. It minimises inventory and exposes problems but needs stable, level demand and reliable processes (developed fully in Lesson 7).

The two are not mutually exclusive, many firms run MRP for planning and pull for execution.

Little's Law

The most general flow relationship is Little's Law (Little, 1961): Inventory (WIP) = Throughput rate x Flow time. It holds for any stable process regardless of distribution, and is invaluable for diagnosis: if WIP is high relative to throughput, flow time is long, work is queuing somewhere. It ties together the capacity, inventory, and scheduling domains of this lesson and links back to the cycle-time and bottleneck analytics of Lesson 4.

Worked calculation

A distributor faces annual demand D = 10,000 units, ordering cost S = $50, holding cost H = $4/unit/year. EOQ = sqrt((2 x 10,000 x 50)/4) = sqrt(250,000) = 500 units, implying 20 orders/year and an average cycle stock of 250 units. Total relevant cost = ordering (20 x 50 = $1,000) + holding (250 x 4 = $1,000) = $2,000, the two costs are equal at the optimum, as the model predicts. For timing: with ~250 working days, daily demand is 40 units; at a 5-day lead time the average lead-time demand is 200 units. If sigma_d = 10 units/day, safety stock at 95% service = 1.65 x 10 x sqrt(5) = ~37 units, giving ROP = 200 + 37 = 237 units. Now suppose the supplier offers a 2% discount for orders of 1,000: compare total cost (purchase + ordering + holding) at Q = 500 versus Q = 1,000, including the lower unit price, and order the larger quantity only if the discount outweighs the extra holding cost, the quantity-discount logic.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Treating utilisation as the goal. Maximising utilisation in a variable system lengthens queues sharply (queuing theory: as utilisation approaches 100%, waiting time explodes), so high utilisation can worsen service.
  • Misreading EOQ as precise. Its value is the order-of-magnitude balance and its insight that cutting setup cost shrinks batches; the flat cost curve means small deviations barely matter.
  • Confusing safety stock with cycle stock. Cycle stock comes from batching (EOQ); safety stock comes from uncertainty (service level). They are sized by different logics.
  • Push/pull dogmatism. Neither MRP nor JIT is universally superior; fit depends on demand stability, BOM complexity, and process reliability.
  • Ignoring Little's Law. Managers add capacity to cut lead time when the real cause is excessive WIP, which Little's Law would have revealed.

Cross-links

This lesson runs the process designed in Lesson 4 (its takt, cycle-time, and bottleneck analytics feed capacity and Little's Law) within the strategic capacity and planning-and-control decision areas of Lesson 3. Its inventory and pull concepts are the direct prelude to lean and JIT (Lesson 7), where setup reduction (SMED) and kanban put the EOQ and reorder logic into motion; demand variability and the bullwhip effect extend into supply chain management (Lesson 6); and the OEE/quality losses connect to quality and six sigma (Lesson 8).
